retrieved_docs.title: Nasoseptal Flap -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: As the flap is raised posteriorly, caution should not injure the pedicle overlying the sphenoid rostrum. Once raised, the flap should be tucked away for safekeeping (most commonly in the nasopharynx, although the maxillary sinus has been described if an antrostomy has been performed). When ready to reconstruct the skull base, it can be withdrawn and replaced along the septum to ensure appropriate orientation and avoid torsion. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Balloon Sinuplasty -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Another landmark trial in the literature, The BREATHE 1 study, by Stankewitz et al., performed only balloon sinuplasty on 58 maxillary ostia with the Entellus FinESS system. They utilized CT imaging of the sinuses to look at patency rates with this new system. They found that 95.8% of the ballooned maxillary sinus ostia at the three-month follow-up appointments were patent via CT imaging. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Nasopharyngeal Airway -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: The NPA must be inserted into the most patent and horizontal nares with the concave side facing down to facilitate insertion into the posterior pharynx. The NPA may be rotated if resistance is present to allow the tube to fit snugly into the nares. Direct the tube in the occiput's direction posteriorly and along the nasal floor. This prevents cephalad positioning and ensures the NPA passes through the lower pathway of the naris.

retrieved_docs.title: Sinus Endoscopic Surgery -- Complications -- Disease Recurrence Disease
retrieved_docs.content: When FESS is performed for CRS, there is always a risk of recurrence or persistence. Failure to perform a proper maxillary antrostomy and widen the natural ostium can lead to mucus recirculation through the accessory ostium.

retrieved_docs.title: Sinus Endoscopic Surgery -- Technique or Treatment -- Maxillary Antrostomy
retrieved_docs.content: The natural ostium of the maxillary sinus is visualized after removing the uncinate. It is elliptical and found in the lower part of the infundibulum. It is crucial to distinguish the natural ostium from the accessory ostium. The opening to the maxillary sinus is confirmed using a ball-tip probe and is best visualized with a 30- or 45-degree scope. Once confirmed, the natural ostium is enlarged using a through cutting instrument, punch forceps, and powered debrider. The ostium is enlarged posteriorly and inferiorly to avoid injury to the orbit superiorly and the nasolacrimal duct anteriorly.

retrieved_docs.title: Dacryocystorhinostomy -- Introduction -- Endoscopic Nasal Anatomy
retrieved_docs.content: The maxillary line is the most medial part of the frontal process of the maxilla, and it runs from the axilla of the middle turbinate along the lateral nasal wall ending at the inferior turbinate. It is an important landmark for the placement of mucosal incisions.

retrieved_docs.title: Choanal Atresia -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The transpalatal approach was the most frequently used method until the development of the endoscopic endonasal approach, which is now the standard of care for nearly all cases. [42] Endoscopic techniques offer improved outcomes with fewer complications compared to traditional open procedures. This approach allows direct visualization of the atretic segment, which is then perforated with rigid instruments or drilled out if bone is present. The overlying mucosa is redraped to ensure the patency of the neochoana. Septal mucosal flaps may be used similarly to maintain this patency. [43]

retrieved_docs.title: Balloon Sinuplasty -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The authors do note the possibility of publication bias in the literature. Four of the larger studies listed in this project were sponsored by Acclarent or Entellus, the two main manufacturers of BSP devices. We hope that with the recent increases in BSP only procedures, more data can be used to study the efficacy of balloon sinuplasty better and allow us to reach a consensus on clinical indications for the procedure. The authors note some discrepancies within the literature concerning complications, especially regarding skull base injury, and hope that more focus can be placed on reporting any suspected BSP-related complications. The authors believe BSP can provide symptomatic improvement, without major complications, in the correctly selected patient population. Although there is some conflict in regard to its indications and clinical use, with its mucosal tissue-sparing properties, it can serve as a great tool in the arsenal of otolaryngologists for patients suffering from chronic sinus disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Fluoroscopy Nasogastric Feeding Tube Placement -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: To fully understand the nasogastric tube placement, it is best to understand the human anatomy that the practitioner traverses during the procedure. Insertion begins at the nares, the outermost part of the nose, also referred to as the nostrils. Once the tube enters the nose, it progresses into the anterior nasal vestibule. The nasal septum, comprised of cartilage, separates the two sides. Beyond the vestibule, the tube has reached the concha. On either side of the concha are the nasal sinuses. This area is approximately 5 to 7 cm posterior to the nares, and it ultimately connects to the nasopharynx posteriorly. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Nasopharyngeal Airway -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: The potential complications of NPA placement and NT intubation include the following: Epistaxis due to nasal mucosal injury Turbinate fracture Turbinectomy Intracranial placement through a basilar skull fracture Retropharyngeal dissection or laceration Sinusitis, which can result in sepsis in the immunocompromised Gastric distension if the NPA is too large or the NT tube is inserted in the esophagus Blind NT intubation increases the risk of esophageal placement and retropharyngeal laceration, but otherwise, blind and bronchoscopic placement have similar complications. [15] NPA placement is absolutely contraindicated when the patient has a basilar skull fracture. This condition risks cephalad NPA insertion and possible damage to brain structures.

retrieved_docs.title: Adenoidectomy -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: A nasal suction catheter is introduced, and its tip is withdrawn from the oropharynx. The 2 ends are then clipped under moderate tension adjacent to the alar cartilage to retract the soft palate anteriorly. A tonsil swab may be positioned between the catheter and nose to prevent pressure necrosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Total Laryngectomy -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: If planned, a tracheoesophageal puncture (TEP) is performed at this time; otherwise, a cricopharyngeal myotomy is performed to maximize postoperative swallowing. If the patient does not have a gastrostomy tube, a nasogastric tube is passed under direct visualization from a naris into the pharynx and into the esophagus. This is sutured to the membranous nasal septum using a 3-0 silk. The pharyngeal defect is closed, preferably in a transverse fashion; if there is sufficient mucosa to close the neo-pharynx over the nasogastric tube, the neo-pharynx will be of sufficient caliber to permit swallowing once healed.

retrieved_docs.title: Barosinusitis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: If patients fail medical management, surgical treatment may be indicated. Treatment with functional endoscopic sinus surgery (FESS) can offer a long-term success rate of 92% to 95%. Recommendations include sphenoethmoidectomy, polypectomy, mucosal resection, maxillary antrostomy, and wide frontal recess clearance (Draf IIA minimum), +/-  turbinate and septal surgery. The modified Lothrop procedure (Draf III) should be reserved as a rescue procedure for recurrent restenosis with associated frontal recess barotrauma. Septoplasty or removal of concha bullosa may be indicated in some patients. Balloon Sinuplasty (BSP) is a relatively new technique that consists of using a high-pressure balloon system to dilate sinus ostia and outflow tracts via local microfracturing - without destroying the overlying mucosa and maintaining physiologic function. The goal of BSP is to allow for improved mucociliary clearance by minimizing obstruction of the outflow tracts thus causing symptomatic improvement in patients suffering from sinusitis. Careful investigation by an otolaryngologist is paramount. Preventive measures include avoidance of flying or diving during head colds and episodes of allergic rhinitis or sinusitis. [1] [13] [14] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Le Fort Osteotomy -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Once down-fractured, additional nasal mucosal dissection or repair of torn nasal mucosa may be more easily performed due to improved access. Repair is best performed with a fine, resorbable suture, such as a 5-0 fast-absorbing gut.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Nasal Concha -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: The turbinates themselves may be used as part of a reconstructive effort. The inferior turbinates are commonly used as regional flaps, based on the blood supply at the anterior face of the turbinate arising from the anterior lateral nasal and anterior ethmoidal arteries. The flaps effectively provide mucosal resurfacing during total or subtotal nasal reconstruction or repair of large septal perforations. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Sinus Endoscopic Surgery -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The external nose consists of nasal bones, upper later cartilages, and lower lateral cartilages. The internal part of the nose is divided into 2 nasal cavities by the septum. Each nasal cavity has a medial wall, a vertical septum, and a lateral wall. The crista galli, cribriform plates, and the sphenoid body form the roof of the nasal cavity. The floor consists of the maxilla's palatine process and the palatine bone's horizontal process. [4]

Separately, nasal endoscopy with maxillary antrostomy is classified as a diagnostic procedure on the nose, mouth, and pharynx, exemplifying the application of endoscopic techniques in both otolaryngology and orthopedic contexts. This integrated framework clarifies which surgical specialties perform specific joint procedures, how those procedures are organized within the overall taxonomy of musculoskeletal surgery, and the common procedural methods that support coordinated patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1929
retrieved_docs.content: Nasal endoscopy with maxillary antrostomy is classified as a diagnostic procedure involving the nose, mouth, and pharynx, providing clinicians with direct endoscopic visualization and access to the maxillary sinus for assessment of sinonasal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1981
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose are medical interventions that address nasal disorders (اِضطِراب أنفي). Within this category, control of epistaxis is identified as a specific type of Procedure on Nose, indicating that managing nosebleeds is a subset of nasal procedural care. This relationship highlights that interventions aimed at controlling epistaxis are directly linked to the broader management of nasal disorders, emphasizing the role of targeted procedural treatment in the evaluation and care of patients with nasal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1807
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose encompass the specific intervention of control of epistaxis, which is classified as a type of nasal procedure. The control of epistaxis is performed directly on the nose (nos), indicating that the nose is the precise anatomical site targeted by this bleeding‑control technique.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1217
retrieved_docs.content: Incision – action is a medical procedure that can be performed using several distinct techniques. One method of Incision – action is تركيب قسطرة فوق العانة, which involves the placement of a suprapubic catheter. Another method of Incision – action is رأب القرنية, a technique that refers to corneal scraping or abrasion. Incision – action may also be carried out by means of Nasal endoscopy with maxillary antrostomy. Nasal endoscopy with maxillary antrostomy is classified as a diagnostic procedure on the nose, mouth, and pharynx. Together, these methods illustrate the breadth of applications for incision‑based interventions across urological, ophthalmic, and otolaryngologic specialties, emphasizing the need to select the appropriate technique for accurate diagnosis and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 31
retrieved_docs.content: the broader category of incision‑action surgical techniques. Incision‑action encompasses procedures such as corneal scraping, nasal endoscopy with maxillary antrostomy, reopening of a facial‑bone osteotomy site (classified under other therapeutic procedures on the musculoskeletal system), male‑genital therapeutic interventions (including prostatolithotomy, prostatotomy, calculus removal, and trans‑urethral prostate incision), and removal of the tympanic membrane (tympanostomy). Myringotomy and drainage of the middle ear are directly related to the middle ear and to tympanostomy, establishing a clear anatomical link between ear surgery and the incision‑action group. Intubation serves as a central procedural theme that connects airway and gastrointestinal interventions. Specific intubation methods include permanent esophageal catheter insertion, tracheal T‑tube placement (a type of tracheal stoma), trans‑glottic tracheal catheterization, laryngeal mask airway tube insertion, and

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 60
retrieved_docs.content: also extends beyond the urinary tract, illustrated by nasal endoscopy with maxillary antrostomy as a diagnostic procedure on the nose, mouth, and pharynx, demonstrating the wide applicability of endoscopic techniques. Overall, the hierarchy links inspection‑based endoscopic methods, imaging modalities, and therapeutic interventions across the urethra, ureter, kidney, and related upper urinary‑tract structures, clarifying coding, classification, and clinical decision‑making for combined surgical and diagnostic urologic procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 211
retrieved_docs.content: nasal endoscopy with maxillary antrostomy is placed in the group of diagnostic procedures that examine the nose, mouth and pharynx. Collectively, these entities and relationships form an “is‑a,” “has‑child,” “has‑parent,” and “method‑of” taxonomy that supports precise clinical classification, interdisciplinary communication, accurate coding, and informed procedural selection in the management of urological, renal, female‑perineal, gastrointestinal and related diagnostic‑therapeutic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 277
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose are associated with nasal disorder (اِضطِراب أنفي).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2576
retrieved_docs.content: Control of epistaxis is a medical procedure whose direct site of action is the nose (nos).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1954
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx are categorized as a sub‑type of عمليات الأنف, indicating that they fall within the broader group of nose‑related surgical interventions. These diagnostic procedures are closely linked to منظار داخلي, an internal endoscopic technique that is commonly employed to visualize and assess the nasal cavity, oral cavity, and pharyngeal structures. Understanding that diagnostic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx belong to عمليات الأنف and utilize منظار داخلي helps clinicians select appropriate endoscopic tools for accurate evaluation and management of upper airway conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 688
retrieved_docs.content: Incision – action is a procedural category that can be carried out by several distinct methods, including تركيب قسطرة فوق العانة (placement of a suprapubic catheter), رأب القرنية (corneal scraping), Nasal endoscopy with maxillary antrostomy, and Reopening of osteotomy site of facial bone. Nasal endoscopy with maxillary antrostomy is classified as a diagnostic procedure on the nose, mouth, and pharynx, indicating its role in evaluating upper‑airway and sinus pathology. Reopening of osteotomy site of facial bone is regarded as a therapeutic intervention within the musculoskeletal system and is closely related to, and possibly synonymous with, other or therapeutic procedures on the musculoskeletal system. The broader category “Other OR therapeutic procedures on musculoskeletal system” serves to classify such musculoskeletal therapeutic actions, linking them to the overall framework of incision‑based interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 259
retrieved_docs.content: and to the neurosurgical and spinal explorations. Additional Excision applications include therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth and pharynx and are related to plastic procedures on the nose. Together, Exploration provides an umbrella framework for targeted surgical investigations, while Excision represents the core operative technique that enables tissue removal, obstruction relief, diagnostic assessment and neural decompression, thereby optimizing patient outcomes across multiple specialties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2834
retrieved_docs.content: The term جراحة (surgery) functions as a primary category that encompasses more specialized procedural types, including ultrazvuková chirurgie (ultrasound‑guided surgery) and البزل (aspiration), each of which is described as having جراحة as its parent. In addition, the concept clinically_associated_with is linked to جراحة and عمليات الأنف (nose surgeries), indicating that clinical associations are recognized between general surgical practice and specific nasal operative interventions. This hierarchical and relational structure highlights how broad surgical disciplines relate to both advanced technique‑specific procedures and particular anatomical applications, informing a comprehensive understanding of surgical taxonomy and its clinical relevance.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 242
retrieved_docs.content: Head and Neck Surgery encompasses procedures classified under بضع طبلة الأذن, indicating that ear‑related interventions are a subspecialty of this surgical field. The generic action of an incision serves as a methodological foundation for a wide range of therapeutic and diagnostic procedures. It is employed in the placement of a catheter above the pubic region (تركيب قسطرة فوق العانة) and in corneal scraping (رأب القرنية), and it underlies both nasal endoscopy with maxillary antrostomy and the reopening of an osteotomy site of the facial bone. The incision action also functions as a method for male genital therapeutic procedures and for ear procedures grouped under بضع طبلة الأذن. Nasal endoscopy with maxillary antrostomy is categorized as a diagnostic procedure of the nose, mouth, and pharynx, illustrating its role in evaluating upper airway and sinus pathology. Reopening of an osteotomy site of the facial bone is linked to other therapeutic procedures on the musculoskeletal system,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2146
retrieved_docs.content: The larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system, indicating that certain respiratory interventions that are not directly therapeutic to the larynx can nonetheless involve the larynx indirectly. This relationship highlights the importance of recognizing the larynx’s involvement when planning and performing non‑therapeutic respiratory procedures, as indirect effects on the larynx may impact airway function and patient outcomes.
